articles.title: Almost half of adults worldwide eat out at least once a week--exacerbating the obesity epidemic
articles.summary_3_sentences: Research presented at ECO 2026 links eating out rather than preparing meals at home with weight gain and obesity across global settings. The item frames frequent out-of-home eating as a population-level contributor to obesity risk. It reports an association rather than proving causation.

articles.title: Pooled analysis reveals semaglutide shows good efficacy in older adults aged over 65 years
articles.summary_3_sentences: A pooled analysis of the STEP trials reported that semaglutide had similar efficacy and safety in adults older than 65 as in the broader trial populations. The analysis included involvement from semaglutide manufacturer Novo Nordisk. The findings support use in older adults but should be interpreted in the context of post-hoc trial analysis.

articles.title: New post-hoc analysis examined daily oral orforglipron in adults over 65 with obesity, with or without diabetes
articles.summary_3_sentences: A post-hoc ECO 2026 analysis found that daily oral orforglipron in adults aged 65 and older showed results and a safety profile similar to the broader ATTAIN clinical program. The report included people with obesity, with or without diabetes. As a subgroup analysis, it adds supportive evidence but does not replace dedicated prospective trials in older adults.

articles.title: Could the 'Ozempic Era' shift blame for obesity from individuals to the food industry?
articles.summary_3_sentences: An essay presented at ECO 2026 argues that the rise of obesity drugs could shift public narratives away from individual blame and toward the food industry. The piece draws parallels with changing views on tobacco and alcohol industries. It is a policy and framing discussion rather than a clinical study.

articles.title: Body mass index: It's not all that it's hyped up to be
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reviews limitations of BMI as a quick health metric, noting that its simplicity can obscure important aspects of body composition and health. It argues that BMI is widely used because it is cheap and easy, not because it captures all relevant risk. The piece is explanatory commentary about measurement limits rather than new trial data.

articles.title: Study shows that a 1% reduction in annual working hours is associated with a 0.16% decrease in obesity rates
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study presented at ECO 2026 found that lower annual working hours across OECD countries were associated with lower obesity rates. The reported estimate was a 0.16% decrease in obesity rates for each 1% reduction in annual working hours. The finding is ecological and associative, pointing to a possible role for work patterns in population health.

articles.title: 8,500 steps a day can help dieters keep weight off, research suggests
articles.summary_3_sentences: Research presented at ECO 2026 suggests that about 8,500 steps per day may help people maintain weight loss after dieting. The report highlights walking as a practical behavioral strategy for weight maintenance. It describes an association from presented research rather than a definitive universal threshold.

articles.title: Over half of parents in England are pestered by their children to buy junk food while food shopping, survey suggests
articles.summary_3_sentences: A nationally representative survey of parents in England found that many are frequently pressured by children to buy foods high in fat, salt, and sugar. Most parents who reported frequent pestering also said they often bought the requested items. The findings point to family and retail environments as contributors to unhealthy purchasing patterns.

articles.title: Study shows benefits in obesity-linked conditions of losing more weight with GLP-1 treatment
articles.summary_3_sentences: Research presented at ECO 2026 reported that greater weight loss with incretin-based treatment was linked to lower risk of obesity-related conditions. The summary also notes that even modest weight loss compared with weight gain was associated with relative benefit. The item describes risk associations after treatment rather than proving direct effects on each condition.

articles.title: The dark side of weight loss drugs: Ozempic's surprising hidden cost
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report says people who lose weight with GLP-1 drugs may face more social judgment than people who lose weight through diet and exercise. It frames the stigma as tied to perceptions that medication is an "easy way out." The item focuses on social attitudes and weight stigma rather than drug efficacy or safety.

articles.title: New obesity discovery rewrites decades of fat science
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers report that hormone-sensitive lipase has a nuclear role in fat cells beyond releasing stored fat. In people and mice lacking this protein, the phenotype described was lipodystrophy rather than expected obesity. The finding is mechanistic and may reshape how adipose biology is understood.

articles.title: New "Trojan horse" obesity drug supercharges weight loss in early tests
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers developed a next-generation obesity therapy that uses GLP-1/GIP targeting to deliver an added metabolic payload into cells. In mice, the compound outperformed existing treatments on appetite, weight loss, and glucose measures. These results are preclinical and do not yet establish performance or safety in humans.

articles.title: Scientists discover why Ozempic works better for some people
articles.summary_3_sentences: A year-long study in Japan suggests that response to GLP-1 drugs may differ depending on why people overeat. People who overate in response to tempting food cues reportedly had better long-term weight and glycemic outcomes than those whose eating was more emotion-driven. The findings point to behavioral heterogeneity in treatment response rather than a single effect for all patients.

articles.title: Men and women with obesity face very different hidden health risks
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report says obesity was associated with different risk patterns in men and women, with men showing more visceral fat and liver stress and women showing higher inflammation and cholesterol levels. The findings suggest that obesity biology and risk expression may differ by sex. The work supports more tailored approaches to assessment and treatment.

articles.title: Why Ozempic doesn't work for everyone: Scientists just found a hidden reason
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers report that some people may respond less well to GLP-1 drugs because of genetic variants linked to so-called GLP-1 resistance. The article suggests this could affect around 10% of patients. The finding is presented as a possible explanation for variable response and may inform future stratified treatment approaches.

articles.title: Simple "gut reset" may stop weight gain after Ozempic or Wegovy
articles.summary_3_sentences: A clinical trial reported that duodenal mucosal resurfacing may reduce weight regain after stopping GLP-1 medications such as semaglutide. The minimally invasive procedure renews the lining of the upper small intestine and is proposed to help preserve metabolic benefits. The findings are promising but need broader validation and longer follow-up.

articles.title: Treatment-resistant depression may yield to combinations of medications already in clinical use
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers reported in JAMA Psychiatry that combinations of medications already in clinical use may help some people with treatment-resistant depression. The article highlights the need for alternatives when current standard approaches fail. It presents a repurposing and combination strategy rather than a brand-new drug class.

articles.title: Like mother, like fetus: Study finds contagious yawning begins in the womb
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study in Current Biology suggests that contagious yawning may begin before birth. The report links fetal yawning responses to broader ideas about social mirroring and early brain development. It is primarily a developmental neuroscience finding rather than a clinical application.

articles.title: Brain protein ASC identified as driver of inflammation after mild head injury
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified the protein ASC as a driver of inflammation after mild traumatic brain injury. The article notes that while many mild TBIs resolve, a substantial minority of patients develop persistent symptoms and longer-term neuropsychiatric risks. The finding points to a specific inflammatory target for future investigation.

articles.title: Using the body's own sounds to diagnose Alzheimer's and Parkinson's before the first symptoms appear
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report describes efforts to use internal body sounds as early indicators of Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease. It emphasizes the possibility of detecting subtle changes before classic symptoms emerge. The article presents an early diagnostic concept rather than a validated screening tool.

articles.title: Ultrasensitive test detects biomarkers for specific form of dementia
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers developed an ultrasensitive biomarker test aimed at detecting frontotemporal lobar degeneration and its subtypes. The article notes that biomarker tools exist for Alzheimer's disease but are more limited for rarer dementias. The new test could improve diagnostic specificity if validated further.

articles.title: Brain scans reveal a shocking difference between psychopaths and other people
articles.summary_3_sentences: MRI-based research found that participants with psychopathic traits had, on average, a larger striatum than comparison participants. The study linked this difference to reward-related and impulsive behavioral traits. It is a structural association study and does not imply a simple deterministic cause of psychopathy.

articles.title: Scientists discover the brain's hidden "stop scratching" switch
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified TRPV4 as part of a neural braking system that limits scratching behavior. In mouse models of chronic itch, animals lacking this signal scratched less often but had trouble stopping once they started. The work is mechanistic and preclinical, with relevance to itch regulation rather than an immediate therapy.

articles.title: MIT scientists discover millions of "silent synapses" in the adult brain
articles.summary_3_sentences: MIT researchers report that adult cortex contains many silent synapses that can be activated during learning. The finding challenges the view that such synapses are mainly restricted to early development. It suggests a latent structural reserve for forming new memories in the adult brain.

articles.title: This simple amino acid supplement greatly reduces Alzheimer's damage
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study reported that oral arginine reduced amyloid buildup, brain inflammation, and behavioral problems in animal models of Alzheimer's disease. The article presents arginine as an inexpensive and already familiar compound. These are preclinical results and should not be interpreted as proof of effectiveness in people.

articles.title: Weight loss drug Ozempic linked to lower depression and anxiety risk
articles.summary_3_sentences: A large observational study linked semaglutide-class drugs to fewer psychiatric hospital visits and sick days. The report suggests a possible mental health benefit alongside metabolic effects. Because the evidence is observational, it does not establish that the drugs directly reduce depression or anxiety risk.

articles.title: This simple blood test might detect depression before symptoms appear
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers linked accelerated aging in monocytes to emotional and cognitive symptoms of depression. The report raises the possibility of a blood-based indicator that could identify risk before full symptoms appear. It is an early biomarker concept rather than a ready-to-use diagnostic test.

articles.title: Alzheimer's drugs may not work and could raise brain risks
articles.summary_3_sentences: A major review of amyloid-beta clearing drugs concluded that these therapies may offer little meaningful benefit while increasing risks such as brain swelling and bleeding. The article summarizes evidence across more than 20,000 participants. It adds to ongoing debate about the balance of benefit and harm in Alzheimer's drug development.

articles.title: Scientists found the brain doesn't start blank, it starts full
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that early hippocampal neural networks begin dense and then become more efficient through pruning. The work challenges the idea that memory circuitry starts from a blank slate. It is a basic neuroscience study of early brain organization.

articles.title: Scientists just discovered what coffee is really doing to your gut and brain
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report says both caffeinated and decaf coffee altered gut bacteria in ways linked to mood and stress, with additional cognitive differences between the two. The article frames coffee as acting through more than caffeine alone. The findings are exploratory and do not establish clinical effects in routine consumers.

articles.title: The creepy feeling in old buildings might have a surprising cause
articles.summary_3_sentences: A small experiment found that infrasound exposure altered mood-related measures and raised cortisol without participants consciously detecting the stimulus. The work suggests that very low-frequency vibration may influence stress and perception. It is an early experimental finding with limited scope.

articles.title: Boosting one protein helps the brain fight Alzheimer's
articles.summary_3_sentences: In mice with Alzheimer's-like pathology, increasing Sox9 boosted astrocyte activity, reduced plaque buildup, and preserved cognition. The study highlights support cells as possible therapeutic targets. These are preclinical findings and not evidence of benefit in humans.

articles.title: Scientists discover a hidden brain "cleaning" effect triggered by movement
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers report that abdominal muscle contractions can subtly move the brain and may help circulate cerebrospinal fluid. The proposed mechanism could contribute to waste clearance in the central nervous system. The finding is physiological and mechanistic, not a proven intervention for disease prevention.

articles.title: New Study on Why SuperAgers Live Longer and Have Better Memories
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that SuperAgers may have multiple mechanisms that preserve memory and support longer life. It presents SuperAgers as a model for studying resilient cognitive aging. The piece is a brief overview rather than a detailed clinical report.

articles.title: A hidden map in your nose could explain how smell works
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers mapped millions of neurons in mice and found that smell receptors in the nose are arranged in structured, overlapping stripes rather than randomly. The layout appears to mirror organization in the brain's smell circuits. This is a basic neuroscience finding in mice that advances understanding of olfaction.

articles.title: Scientists restore memory by blocking a single Alzheimer's protein
articles.summary_3_sentences: A mouse study found that blocking PTP1B improved memory and helped brain immune cells clear plaque. Because PTP1B is also linked to diabetes and obesity, the work suggests a shared pathway across conditions. The results are preclinical and do not establish a human treatment.

articles.title: Scientists may have found the brain's switch for chronic pain
articles.summary_3_sentences: Animal research identified the caudal granular insular cortex as a region that may help sustain chronic pain after injury. Shutting down the pathway reportedly prevented chronic pain formation and could reverse it in the model. The work is preclinical and points to a possible neural target for future therapies.

articles.title: This hidden kind of stress may be damaging your memory as you age
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study in older Chinese Americans linked internalized stress, especially hopelessness, to faster memory decline. The report suggests that culturally specific stressors may contribute to underrecognized cognitive risk. It supports attention to mental health and culturally sensitive prevention strategies.

articles.title: How an Inability to Integrate New Info Underlies Schizophrenia
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article describes research suggesting that impaired integration of new information may underlie key features of schizophrenia. It frames the disorder in terms of disrupted cognitive processing rather than a single symptom domain. The summary is brief but points to a mechanistic account of the illness.

articles.title: Harvard scientists link gut bacteria to depression through hidden inflammation trigger
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers linked a gut bacterium and an environmental pollutant to production of an inflammatory molecule associated with depression. The work provides a possible molecular pathway connecting microbiome activity, immune signaling, and mood. It is mechanistic research rather than a clinical intervention study.

articles.title: For the first time, scientists pinpoint the brain cells behind depression
articles.summary_3_sentences: By analyzing donated human brain tissue, researchers identified two cell types that differed in people with depression. The changes involved neurons associated with mood and stress as well as microglia-related immune pathways. The study strengthens the biological basis of depression and may guide future targeted treatments.

articles.title: These 80-year-olds have the memory of 50-year-olds. Scientists now know why
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article profiles SuperAgers, older adults whose memory remains unusually strong despite age. It suggests that resilience to or tolerance of Alzheimer's-related pathology, along with social and biological factors, may underlie the phenotype. The work offers clues for healthier cognitive aging rather than a direct intervention.

articles.title: An experimental new drug for stiff person syndrome restores mobility
articles.summary_3_sentences: Science News reports that CAR T cell therapy improved walking speed in people with stiff person syndrome by targeting the source of pathogenic antibodies. The treatment is experimental and applied to a rare autoimmune neurological disorder. The story highlights a potentially important proof of concept for immune-targeted therapy in severe disease.

articles.title: Suicide deaths in U.S. teens and young adults fell after 988 launch
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study highlighted by Science News found a link between launch of the 988 Lifeline and lower suicide mortality in teens and young adults. The finding suggests that access to crisis support may have measurable public health effects. Because the evidence is population-level, it supports association rather than direct proof for each individual outcome.

articles.title: Scientists say this type of olive oil could boost brain power
articles.summary_3_sentences: A two-year study found that extra virgin olive oil was associated with better cognition and greater gut microbial diversity than refined olive oil. The report suggests a gut-brain pathway may contribute to the difference. It is a nutrition-focused study that suggests benefit but does not establish broad clinical prevention claims.

articles.title: Activities Like Reading May Reduce Alzheimer's Risk by 38%
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article says intellectually stimulating activities such as reading and writing are associated with lower Alzheimer's disease risk. It presents cognitive engagement as a potentially protective lifestyle factor. The report describes association rather than proving that the activities directly prevent disease.

articles.title: Doing this throughout life may cut Alzheimer's risk by 38%
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report says lifelong cognitive enrichment, including reading and learning, was associated with lower Alzheimer's risk and later symptom onset. It supports the idea that mentally stimulating activities may build resilience. The evidence is observational and does not prove a direct protective effect for every activity.

articles.title: Lonely people have worse memory but don't decline faster, study finds
articles.summary_3_sentences: A long-term European study found that loneliness was associated with poorer baseline memory but not faster decline over time. The results challenge the idea that loneliness necessarily accelerates dementia-related deterioration. They suggest loneliness may affect current cognitive performance more than slope of decline.

articles.title: Vitamin D in 30's and 40's May Protect Against Dementia
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that higher vitamin D levels in midlife were linked to lower tau levels later in life. It suggests a possible relationship between vitamin D status and later dementia-related biology. The piece is brief and describes an association rather than a prevention trial.

articles.title: Your nose could detect Alzheimer's years before symptoms begin
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that immune cells may damage smell-related nerve fibers early in Alzheimer's disease, before cognitive symptoms are obvious. The work suggests smell changes could serve as an early indicator of disease. It is a mechanistic and diagnostic lead, not a validated screening program.

articles.title: Potential Rare Human-to-Human Transmission in a Hantavirus Outbreak
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article discusses the possibility of rare person-to-person transmission in a recent hantavirus outbreak linked to a cruise ship. It highlights uncertainty around how the outbreak spread and why the event drew attention. The piece is a news overview rather than a definitive epidemiologic conclusion.

articles.title: Hantavirus on small cruise ship may have been spread by human contact
articles.summary_3_sentences: Medical Xpress reports that the cruise ship hantavirus outbreak may have involved spread through human contact. The story emphasizes the severity of illness and the need for caution while the outbreak is investigated. It presents an emerging public health concern with transmission mode still under evaluation.

articles.title: Hantavirus-stricken cruise ship arrives at Tenerife in Spain's Canary Islands
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report covers the arrival of a cruise ship affected by a hantavirus outbreak at Tenerife, where passengers and some crew were expected to disembark. The article focuses on the operational and public health response to the incident. It documents the event rather than presenting new biomedical findings.

articles.title: Everyone on hantavirus-hit ship 'high-risk contact', must be monitored: WHO
articles.summary_3_sentences: WHO classified everyone on the affected cruise ship as high-risk contacts requiring active monitoring for 42 days. The report underscores the seriousness of the outbreak and the precautionary public health approach being taken. It is a policy and surveillance update tied to the same event.

articles.title: Uncloaking a Sleeping Sickness Mystery
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article highlights research into a longstanding mystery in sleeping sickness, or trypanosomiasis. It points to mechanistic advances in understanding a neglected tropical disease. The summary is limited, but it clearly refers to a concrete biomedical research development.

articles.title: Soil Microbes Help Reduce the Risk Pathogens Pose to Humans
articles.summary_3_sentences: This piece reports that soil microbial communities may help reduce risks posed by pathogens to humans. It frames human health within broader ecological interactions among microbes, plants, and animals. The article is general but still describes a specific scientific finding on pathogen ecology.

articles.title: Scientists find natural compounds that hit COVID-19 from every angle
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified plant-derived galloylquinic acids that acted against SARS-CoV-2 at multiple stages in experimental systems. The compounds reportedly affected viral entry, replication, and inflammatory responses. These findings are preclinical and do not establish a treatment for COVID-19 in patients.

articles.title: Scientists sound alarm as dangerous amoebas spread globally
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article warns that free-living amoebae may become a larger health problem as warming temperatures and aging water systems expand risk. Some species can directly infect humans and can also shelter other microbes. The report emphasizes surveillance and water treatment needs rather than a single outbreak.

articles.title: Updates on the Growing Problem of Fungal Infections
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article summarizes concerns about the growing global burden of invasive fungal infections. It notes that millions of cases occur yearly and implies that the problem remains underrecognized. The piece is a concise overview of an important infectious disease trend.

articles.title: How the Global Wildlife Trade Harms Humans
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report describes the wildlife trade as a source of human health harms, including disease risks tied to animal movement and contact. It frames the issue as both ecological and public health related. The piece is policy-relevant even though it is not focused on one specific outbreak.

articles.title: Graphene kills harmful bacteria "superbugs" but spares human cells
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers report that graphene oxide can selectively damage bacterial membranes while sparing human cells. The article says the material worked against drug-resistant bacteria and may aid wound healing. These are experimental findings and not yet a clinically approved antimicrobial approach.

articles.title: How climate change may increase antibiotic resistance
articles.summary_3_sentences: Science News reports that heat and drought may promote bacterial exchange of antibiotic resistance genes. The piece links climate-related environmental stress to a human health threat. It is a mechanistic and public health story about how environmental change may reshape resistance dynamics.

articles.title: Two Antibotic-Resistant Pathogens are on the Rise
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that two antibiotic-resistant pathogens are increasing and reiterates longstanding warnings about resistant infections. It is a concise surveillance-style update on antimicrobial resistance. The details are limited, but the item clearly concerns a specific health trend.

articles.title: Scientists discover skincare compound that kills drug-resistant bacteria
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that madecassic acid, a compound used in skincare products, inhibited drug-resistant bacteria including E. coli. The compound was reported to target a bacterial protein absent in humans. The work is preclinical and points to a possible antibiotic lead rather than an established therapy.

articles.title: SNIPE - A Bacterial Defense Against Viral Invaders
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article covers a bacterial defense system called SNIPE that acts against viral invaders. It highlights the continuing microbial arms race between bacteria and phages. The finding is basic microbiology with potential relevance to biotechnology and microbial ecology.

articles.title: Scientists discover bacteria can "explode" to spread antibiotic resistance
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that bacteria can use gene transfer agents to distribute DNA, including resistance-related material, after cells burst open. The study identified a three-gene control module involved in this process. It is a mechanistic finding that improves understanding of how resistance traits may spread.

articles.title: An Eye Disease Transmitted by Seafood Seems to be Emerging
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports concern that a seafood-associated virus, CMNV, may be linked to an emerging eye disease risk. The piece suggests cross-species or foodborne relevance beyond aquatic animals. It is an early warning-style report rather than a definitive outbreak investigation.

articles.title: How Diet & The Gut Microbiome Intersect to Boost Cancer
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article discusses how diet and gut microbiome interactions may contribute to colorectal cancer risk. It frames the microbiome as a mediator between environmental exposures and cancer biology. The item is a mechanistic overview relevant to cancer prevention research.

articles.title: Not just insulin: Early increases in glucagon in type 2 diabetes are linked to fatty liver disease
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study from the German Diabetes Center found that glucagon levels are elevated early in type 2 diabetes and linked to fatty liver disease. The work broadens attention beyond insulin alone in early metabolic dysfunction. It suggests glucagon-related pathways may be important in disease development and monitoring.

articles.title: AI-powered electrocardiogram detects early signs of heart failure
articles.summary_3_sentences: An AI-enabled ECG approach accurately screened patients in Kenya for a precursor of heart failure, according to a JAMA Cardiology study. The report highlights the potential of low-cost ECG infrastructure augmented by algorithms. It suggests a scalable screening strategy, especially in settings with limited access to advanced imaging.

articles.title: New survey finds that majority of Americans are concerned about high blood pressure, the 'silent killer'
articles.summary_3_sentences: A Morning Consult survey found that most Americans are concerned about high blood pressure. The article notes the large population burden of hypertension and rising prevalence even among children. It is a public awareness item rather than new mechanistic or treatment research.

articles.title: Erythritol Affects Brain Cells, May Increase Stroke Risk
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that erythritol affects brain cells in ways that may increase stroke risk. It presents the sweetener as a possible contributor to vascular harm at a cellular level. The report is brief and mechanistic, not a direct clinical outcomes study.

articles.title: A common constipation drug shows surprising power to protect kidneys
articles.summary_3_sentences: A clinical trial in 150 patients found that lubiprostone helped preserve kidney function in moderate chronic kidney disease. The article links the effect to gut microbiome changes that increased spermidine and supported mitochondrial health. The findings suggest a gut-kidney pathway worth further clinical study.

articles.title: Scientists find a way to stop dangerous belly fat as we age
articles.summary_3_sentences: In older women recovering from hip fracture, testosterone gel plus exercise reportedly reduced the usual rise in visceral fat. The article frames abdominal fat redistribution as an important aging-related risk. The finding is specific to a recovery context and should not be generalized beyond the studied group without caution.

articles.title: This common sleep habit could double your risk of heart attack
articles.summary_3_sentences: Long-term follow-up data linked highly irregular bedtimes, especially with shorter sleep, to a roughly doubled risk of major cardiovascular events. The report suggests that sleep regularity may matter as much as duration for heart health. It is an observational finding rather than proof that schedule stabilization alone prevents events.

articles.title: New treatment cuts bad cholesterol by nearly 50% without statins
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers developed DNA-based molecules that target PCSK9 to reduce LDL cholesterol. The article reports reductions approaching 50%, positioning the approach as an alternative to statins. The item presents an emerging therapeutic platform that will require further testing for durability and safety.

articles.title: Why drinking more water didn't prevent kidney stones
articles.summary_3_sentences: A large study tested whether a technology-supported hydration program could reduce recurrence of kidney stones and found it did not achieve the hoped-for benefit. The intervention included smart bottles, reminders, coaching, and incentives. The result suggests that increasing water-related support alone may not be sufficient for recurrence prevention.

articles.title: Two common drugs may reverse fatty liver disease, study finds
articles.summary_3_sentences: In animal models, combining two existing drugs reduced liver fat and may also lower cardiovascular risk markers. The report says lower-dose combination therapy matched the benefit of higher doses alone. These are preclinical findings that still need human testing before clinical conclusions can be drawn.

articles.title: This cheap drug could help people with type 1 diabetes use less insulin
articles.summary_3_sentences: A clinical trial found that metformin use in type 1 diabetes allowed patients to use less insulin while maintaining stable glycemic control. The original expectation was improvement in insulin resistance, but the main practical effect was lower insulin requirement. The findings suggest an adjunctive role that warrants further study in selected patients.

articles.title: Public education key in new cervical cancer screening
articles.summary_3_sentences: A Canadian survey found that many women do not yet understand or trust the transition from Pap testing to HPV-based cervical screening. The study explored screening preferences and communication needs during implementation. The findings highlight that successful screening modernization depends on public education and trust, not only test performance.

articles.title: Nursing-Led Intervention to Ease Anxiety and Depression in Bladder Cancer Survivors
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article describes a nursing-led intervention aimed at reducing anxiety and depression in bladder cancer survivors. It highlights psychosocial care as an important part of survivorship. The summary is brief, but the item clearly concerns a concrete supportive care study.

articles.title: Doctors warn this popular vitamin may quietly disrupt cancer care
articles.summary_3_sentences: Experts warn that biotin supplements may interfere with laboratory tests used in cancer care, potentially obscuring recurrence or delaying decisions. The article also notes limited evidence that biotin helps cancer-related hair loss. It is a clinical caution about supplement use during oncology treatment and follow-up.

articles.title: Colon cancer breakthrough keeps patients cancer-free for nearly 3 years
articles.summary_3_sentences: A UK-led trial reported durable cancer-free outcomes after short-course pembrolizumab before surgery in a defined colorectal cancer subgroup. Patients remained cancer-free for nearly three years in the report. The findings are notable but apply to a specific tumor context rather than all colon cancers.

articles.title: First-ever 3D view shows how killer T cells destroy cancer
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers captured a detailed 3D view of how cytotoxic T cells form an organized contact zone to kill cancer cells. The work improves understanding of antitumor immune function at the molecular level. It is a basic and translational immunology advance rather than a clinical trial result.

articles.title: Vitamin D boosts breast cancer treatment success by 79%
articles.summary_3_sentences: A small study suggested that adding low-dose vitamin D during chemotherapy was associated with higher rates of apparent tumor disappearance in women with breast cancer. The report also notes that vitamin D deficiency is common. Because the study was small, the findings should be viewed as preliminary and hypothesis-generating.

articles.title: Pesticide exposure linked to 150% higher cancer risk in major study
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report says that living in pesticide-heavy environments was associated with markedly higher cancer risk in a major study. The article emphasizes that mixtures of chemicals may matter more than considering each one in isolation. It describes an environmental risk association rather than a direct mechanistic proof for every exposure scenario.

articles.title: Hidden virus in your gut may be linked to colon cancer
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found a virus within a common gut bacterium that may help explain the bacterium's link to colorectal cancer. The work points to bacterial-viral interactions as a factor in cancer risk. It could inform future biomarker or screening strategies if validated further.

topic_groups.topic: Pregnancy, Women's Health, and Early Life

articles.title: What endometriosis means for pregnancy: Data show small rise in babies' congenital anomaly risk
articles.summary_3_sentences: New research in the Canadian Medical Association Journal found a small but significant increase in congenital anomaly risk among babies born to people with endometriosis. The article presents this as an elevated relative risk rather than a statement that most pregnancies are affected. It adds to evidence that endometriosis may have implications beyond pain and fertility.

articles.title: New tool measures whether lactating mothers' psychological needs are being met
articles.summary_3_sentences: A researcher developed the Lactation Psychological Needs Scale to assess the psychological experience of breastfeeding. The tool is intended to measure whether important psychological needs are being met during lactation. It may support research and care focused on maternal well-being during breastfeeding.

articles.title: Baby Microbiomes are Nourished by Breast Milk Sugars
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that breast milk sugars help shape the infant microbiome early in life. It highlights breast milk as a biological input for microbial development rather than only a source of calories. The item summarizes early-life microbiome science with relevance to infant health.

articles.title: This dangerous pregnancy complication is common. A new treatment might help
articles.summary_3_sentences: Science News reports that a blood-filtering treatment lowered blood pressure and prolonged some pregnancies in a recent preeclampsia trial. The condition affects a substantial share of pregnancies and has limited treatment options beyond delivery. The therapy appears promising, but the report indicates that more evaluation is still needed.

articles.title: Prenatal surgery for spina bifida may get a boost from stem cells
articles.summary_3_sentences: A clinical trial suggests that a stem cell-supported prenatal surgery approach for spina bifida is safe so far. Its effectiveness is still being evaluated. The story highlights a translational effort to improve fetal surgery outcomes, but not a confirmed efficacy result yet.

articles.title: Australia has the world's highest rate of ACL reconstruction surgery--rehab may be just as good
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article says Australia has very high rates of ACL reconstruction and argues that rehabilitation alone may be as effective for some patients. It challenges assumptions that surgery is always necessary after ACL injury. The piece points toward more selective decision-making around operative care.

articles.title: Scientists say this simple music trick can boost workout endurance by 20%
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study found that cyclists lasted nearly 20% longer when exercising with self-selected music than in silence. Participants did not feel more exhausted at the end despite the longer effort. The finding suggests music choice may be a simple way to improve exercise adherence or performance.

articles.title: Common knee surgery found ineffective, may make things worse
articles.summary_3_sentences: A 10-year clinical trial found that arthroscopic trimming of a damaged meniscus offered no meaningful benefit over placebo surgery and may lead to worse long-term outcomes. Reported downsides included more symptoms, poorer function, faster osteoarthritis progression, and additional surgery. The findings strongly question routine use of this common procedure in the studied setting.

articles.title: You don't need intense workouts to build muscle, new study reveals
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that slow, controlled eccentric movements can improve strength with relatively low effort. The article suggests that even short daily sessions of simple exercises may help build muscle. It supports accessible strength training approaches for the general public.

articles.title: Scientists reveal the best exercise for knee arthritis pain relief
articles.summary_3_sentences: A review of 217 trials concluded that aerobic exercise was the most effective exercise type for knee osteoarthritis pain and function. Strength and mind-body exercise also helped but were best seen as complementary. The report reinforces exercise as a safe core treatment for knee arthritis.

articles.title: This one change to your exercise routine could add years to your life
articles.summary_3_sentences: Long-term research involving more than 100,000 people suggested that variety in physical activity is associated with lower mortality risk. The benefits appeared to plateau after a certain range rather than increase indefinitely. The finding supports diversified movement patterns as part of healthy aging.

articles.title: Nasal spray developed for prehospital emergency aid for ischemic stroke
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers at the University of Hong Kong developed a nanopowder nasal spray designed to deliver medication to the brain without injection or surgery. The concept is intended for very early emergency treatment of ischemic stroke before hospital arrival. It is a translational technology report and not yet evidence of established community use.

articles.title: Can AI help doctors avoid missed diagnoses? A new study suggests yes
articles.summary_3_sentences: Science News reports that AI may help clinicians reduce missed diagnoses. The story also stresses that real-world validation and human oversight remain necessary before such tools guide patient care routinely. It presents cautious optimism about diagnostic support rather than replacement of clinicians.

articles.title: This AI knew the answers but didn't understand the questions
articles.summary_3_sentences: New research challenges claims that the AI model Centaur truly captures human cognition across many tasks. The critique suggests the system may rely on memorized patterns rather than generalized understanding. The article is relevant to how cognitive AI models should be interpreted in research and applied settings.

articles.title: MIT scientists turn chaotic laser light into powerful brain imaging tool
articles.summary_3_sentences: MIT researchers found conditions under which chaotic laser light forms a focused beam, enabling faster 3D imaging of the blood-brain barrier. The method reportedly achieved much higher speed than existing approaches. The work is a technical imaging advance with potential neuroscience and biomedical applications.

articles.title: AI Models Mirror Human Logic on Real-World Scenarios
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports on research suggesting AI models can align with human logic on real-world scenarios. It addresses the ability of models to distinguish fact from fiction in applied reasoning tasks. The summary is brief, but it clearly refers to a concrete study presentation.

articles.title: CNS-3D Brain Organoids Predict Clinical Seizure Liability
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article describes use of 3D brain organoids to predict seizure liability during drug development. It addresses a major central nervous system safety problem in preclinical screening. The work is translational, aiming to improve prediction of human-relevant adverse effects before clinical trials.

articles.title: This simple 3-amino acid trick boosts mRNA therapy 20-fold
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that adding three common amino acids to lipid nanoparticles greatly increased mRNA delivery and CRISPR editing efficiency in early studies. The improvement appears to come from better cellular uptake rather than changes to the cargo itself. The results are preclinical but could improve future gene therapy platforms.

articles.title: Artificial neurons successfully communicate with living brain cells
articles.summary_3_sentences: Engineers created printed artificial neurons that generated lifelike signals and communicated with living mouse brain cells. The devices are flexible and low cost, pointing to future brain-machine interface possibilities. The finding is early-stage bioengineering rather than a clinical neuroprosthetic application.

articles.title: Cutting calories by 10% to 15% may boost healthy aging without extreme diets
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article says modest calorie reduction of about 10% to 15% may support healthier aging without extreme dieting. It contrasts moderate restriction with more aggressive biohacking approaches. The piece presents a pragmatic longevity-related message rather than a new therapeutic intervention.

articles.title: Creatine Supplements Benefit Muscles, Brain, and Overall Health
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that creatine supplementation may support muscle, brain, and broader health functions. It emphasizes roles beyond athletic performance alone. The piece is a general research summary rather than a single definitive clinical trial.

articles.title: Travel May Slow Aging and Improve Health
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article says travel may help slow aging and improve health. It presents the idea that travel-related activity and experiences could influence overall well-being. The report is brief and should be interpreted as exploratory rather than a clinical prescription.

articles.title: How APOE4 Disrupts the Brain Before Memory Loss
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article discusses how the APOE4 genetic variant may alter brain biology before memory problems become apparent. It frames APOE4 as acting early in the disease process rather than only after symptoms emerge. The item is a mechanistic genetics-focused overview.

articles.title: Revealing the Internal Structure of Cellular Condensates
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article covers new work on the internal organization of cellular condensates, which help cells separate and organize molecules. The finding advances basic cell biology of phase-separated structures. It is mechanistic research with broad relevance across biology.

articles.title: Novel ALS Variants ID'ed, Now 1 in 4 Patients Have Genetic Cause
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that newly identified ALS variants increase the share of patients with a known genetic cause to about one in four. The finding expands the genetic architecture of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. It may have implications for diagnosis, counseling, and future targeted research.

articles.title: Scientists successfully transfer longevity gene and extend lifespan
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers transferred a longevity-related naked mole rat gene into mice and reported longer lifespan and healthier aging features. The modified mice also showed lower inflammation and greater tumor resistance. These are preclinical animal findings and do not imply near-term human anti-aging use.

articles.title: Scientists reversed liver aging with young gut bacteria in stunning study
articles.summary_3_sentences: In mice, restoring a preserved youthful microbiome reduced liver inflammation, DNA damage, and liver cancer signs in older animals. The work linked the effect to suppression of a cancer-associated gene. This is a preclinical aging and microbiome study, not a proven human intervention.

articles.title: What scientists found inside coral reefs could change the future of medicine
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that different coral species host distinct microbial communities producing diverse chemical compounds. The work points to coral reef microbiomes as a potential source of future medical and biotech leads. It is a biodiversity and discovery-oriented basic science report.

articles.title: Scientists accidentally discover DNA that breaks the rules of life
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers discovered a protist with an unusual genetic code that alters how gene stop signals are interpreted. The finding challenges assumptions about the near-universality of genetic translation rules. It is a fundamental biology discovery with no immediate clinical claim.

articles.title: Eating eggs could cut Alzheimer's risk by 27%
articles.summary_3_sentences: This report says older adults who ate eggs more regularly had lower Alzheimer's disease risk in an observational study. Benefits were seen even with modest intake, according to the article. The finding is associative and should not be interpreted as proof that eggs alone prevent dementia.

articles.title: Your DNA may predict your future success more than your upbringing
articles.summary_3_sentences: A twin study reported that genetically influenced differences in IQ were strongly associated with later education, income, and career outcomes. The article argues that outcomes can diverge even among twins raised in the same household. The finding concerns statistical associations and does not reduce life outcomes to genes alone.

articles.title: Evolution isn't random. Scientists find the same genes used for 120 million years
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that distant butterflies and moths reused the same gene pair for warning color patterns over very long evolutionary time. The work suggests that evolution can repeatedly draw on the same genetic toolkit. It is a basic evolutionary biology finding with no direct medical claim.

articles.title: Scientists say travel could slow aging and boost your health
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article frames positive travel experiences as potentially supportive of resilience, metabolism, and stress recovery. It also notes that stressful or unsafe travel could negate those benefits. The report is conceptual and exploratory, not a clinical anti-aging intervention study.

articles.title: Are your memories real? Physicists revisit the Boltzmann brain paradox
articles.summary_3_sentences: This physics-focused article revisits the Boltzmann brain paradox and questions assumptions about memory, time, and entropy. It is a theoretical discussion rather than a biomedical study. The piece is included as a concrete science news item outside direct medical research.

articles.title: Scientists just found a chilling way life may have begun
articles.summary_3_sentences: Experiments suggest freeze-thaw cycles on early Earth may have promoted fusion of primitive lipid compartments and capture of DNA. The work offers a plausible route for increasing chemical complexity before life emerged. It is origin-of-life basic science, not medical research.

articles.title: MIT study finds children more vulnerable to cancer-causing chemical in water
articles.summary_3_sentences: In mouse experiments, young animals exposed to NDMA developed more DNA damage and cancer than adults after similar exposure. The report suggests rapidly dividing cells may make children more vulnerable to this contaminant. These are animal data with implications for environmental risk assessment.

articles.title: Scientists finally solve mystery of strange "golden orb" found 2 miles deep
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers determined that a mysterious deep-sea golden orb was tissue from a giant anemone rather than an egg or sponge. The case shows how DNA sequencing and microscopy can resolve unusual biological findings. It is a biodiversity science story rather than a medical one.

articles.title: DNA research just rewrote the origin of human species
articles.summary_3_sentences: Genetic analyses suggest early humans may have emerged from multiple connected African populations rather than a single ancestral group. The work integrates modern genetic diversity and fossil evidence. It reshapes a major question in human evolutionary history.

articles.title: Giant prehistoric insects didn't need high oxygen after all, study finds
articles.summary_3_sentences: This study challenges the idea that high atmospheric oxygen alone explained giant prehistoric insects. Researchers found insect flight muscles may not have been constrained by oxygen in the expected way. The work redirects debate toward other evolutionary explanations.

articles.title: Scientists just found what keeps plant cells from growing out of control
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified a role for PEX11 in controlling peroxisome size during early plant growth. Altering key genes produced oversized peroxisomes, while a yeast protein restored balance. The study is basic cell biology with cross-species mechanistic interest.

articles.title: Scientists find perfect fossils in rust beneath Australian farmland
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists described exceptionally preserved fossils from McGraths Flat in Australia, preserved in iron-rich material rather than typical sedimentary rock. The site captures fine cellular and anatomical details across species. It is a paleontology discovery of broad scientific interest.

articles.title: 289-million-year-old mummified reptile reveals how breathing began on land
articles.summary_3_sentences: A mummified reptile fossil revealed an early rib-based breathing system resembling that used by modern land vertebrates. The study sheds light on how respiration adapted during vertebrate transition to land. It is an evolutionary anatomy finding rather than a medical study.

articles.title: Advanced Genetic Tools ID a Novel Neurodevelopmental Syndrome
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports identification of a novel neurodevelopmental syndrome using advanced genetic tools. It highlights the value of genomic analysis for diagnosing rare developmental disorders. The brief summary still points to a concrete genetics discovery.

articles.title: Life on Mars? Tiny cells just survived shock waves and toxic soil
articles.summary_3_sentences: In laboratory simulations, yeast cells survived Martian-like shock waves and perchlorate-rich conditions by forming protective molecular clusters. The work suggests a possible general strategy for cellular survival under extreme stress. It is astrobiology research, not evidence of life on Mars.

articles.title: A 67-year-old "crazy" theory about vitamin B1 has finally been proven
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers stabilized a highly reactive molecule in water, supporting a long-standing theory about vitamin B1 chemistry. The finding also has implications for greener synthetic chemistry. It is a basic chemical biology advance rather than a clinical nutrition study.

articles.title: This strange "pearling" motion inside cells could change how we understand disease
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists found that transient mitochondrial pearling helps distribute mitochondrial DNA clusters more evenly. The work adds a new dynamic process to understanding mitochondrial organization. It is mechanistic cell biology with possible relevance to disease but no direct clinical claim.

articles.title: Light makes plants stronger but also holds them back
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified a light-driven mechanism that strengthens adhesion between plant tissues while also limiting expansion. The finding shows that light can simultaneously promote and restrain growth. It is a basic plant biology discovery.

articles.title: Microdosing Cannabis More Popular than Psychedelics
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that microdosing cannabis is almost twice as common as microdosing psychedelics in the United States. It presents a comparative usage trend rather than a treatment recommendation. The item reflects changing patterns of substance use behavior.

articles.title: Young Adults Face Major Cannabis Risk Knowledge Gaps
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article says young adults have major gaps in understanding cannabis-related risks. It points to a mismatch between use and awareness of potential harms. The item is a public health-oriented research summary on cannabis education.

articles.title: Don't toss cannabis leaves: Scientists found rare compounds with medical potential
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified numerous previously unreported compounds in cannabis leaves, including rare flavoalkaloids. The findings expand understanding of cannabis chemistry and suggest possible future biomedical leads. This is a discovery-oriented chemistry study, not evidence of clinical benefit.

articles.title: Cannabis with Opioids Offer No Pain Relief
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reports that adding cannabis to opioids did not improve acute pain relief in patients with knee arthritis. The finding argues against assuming combination benefit in this setting. It is a concise clinical evidence update on pain management.

articles.title: Is Cannabis Effective for Treating Mental Health Disorders?
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article states that current evidence does not support cannabis for treating mental health conditions and instead points to meaningful risks. It presents a cautionary view based on available evidence. The item is relevant to both clinical care and public messaging.

topic_groups.topic: Public Health, Behavior, and Health Communication

articles.title: Protecting pollinating insects could improve diets and livelihoods worldwide
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article links pollinator protection to improved diets and livelihoods, especially in vulnerable agricultural communities. It highlights the food-system and nutrition consequences of ecological decline. The piece connects environmental stewardship with human health and economic well-being.

articles.title: 'Decision fatigue' could be hurting your health. A nutritionist explains
articles.summary_3_sentences: This explanatory article discusses how decision fatigue can make healthy eating choices harder in everyday settings. It focuses on practical barriers rather than lack of knowledge alone. The piece is health communication content grounded in behavioral nutrition concepts.

articles.title: Health advice is all over social media. Here's how to vet claims
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article offers guidance on evaluating health claims encountered on social media. It addresses the mix of useful information and misinformation common on these platforms. The piece is a public-facing health literacy item rather than original research.

articles.title: Why weight gain fears matter in recovery: 10-week program helps women build healthier habits
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study of 607 women in substance use treatment found that a 10-week program improved nutrition, physical activity, intuitive eating, and body image measures. It also reduced body dissatisfaction, thin-ideal internalization, and disordered eating behaviors. The findings suggest that addressing weight-related fears may support recovery-related health behaviors.

articles.title: Scientists reveal creatine's hidden power beyond muscle gains
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article summarizes evidence that creatine supports ATP buffering in muscle, brain, and heart tissue and may affect cognition and mood in some groups. It presents creatine as broader than a sports supplement alone. The report is an overview rather than a single new clinical trial.

articles.title: The people you live with could be changing your gut bacteria
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study in island birds found that stronger social ties were associated with more shared gut microbes, especially those spread by direct contact. The work suggests that everyday social contact can shape microbiome composition. The article extrapolates possible relevance to human households, though the research itself was not in humans.

articles.title: Unusual airborne toxin detected in the U.S. for the first time
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists detected toxic MCCPs in air samples in the Western Hemisphere for the first time. The article points to sewage sludge-derived fertilizer as a likely source. It is an environmental exposure finding with potential public health implications.

articles.title: The dirtiest thing in public bathrooms isn't the toilet seat
articles.summary_3_sentences: This article reviews hygiene risks in public bathrooms and notes that high-touch surfaces and toilet plumes may matter more than toilet seats themselves. It emphasizes airborne droplets and hand hygiene as practical concerns. The piece is a public health explainer rather than a single new study.

excluded_items.title: _Beyond Inheritance_ offers a new view of mutations
CBD Helps Increase BMI of Anorexia Patients
Cyanobacteria Find Another Use for DNA
Hopelessness Speeds Up Memory Loss in Older Asian Americans
